coredata.dc:title: Bio-based waterborne polyurethane/carbon dot nanocomposite as a surface coating material
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Biplab
Gogoi, Satyabrat
Thakur, Suman
Karak, Niranjan
coredata.dc:description: Waterborne surface coating materials with low volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are environmentally friendly and hence the most desired materials for the modern society. The present study reports in situ fabricated thermosetting hyperbranched waterborne polyurethane (WPU)/carbon dot (CD) nanocomposites as surface coating materials. CD was used as a nanoreinforcing agent (at 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 weight percentages) in the nanocomposites. The structures of the waterborne polyurethane and its fabricated thermosetting nanocomposites were analyzed by using different spectroscopic and analytical tools like Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy and X-ray diffractometry. The mechanical properties of the pristine thermosetting WPU were significantly improved after incorporation of CD (tensile strength from 4.5 to 8.5MPa, elongation at break value from 96 to 136%, scratch hardness from 3 to 9kg, impact strength from 70 to 100cm). The thermal stability of WPU also enhanced from 250 to 280°C after incorporation of CD in the matrix. Interestingly, the fabricated nanocomposites demonstrated an excitation wavelength and concentration dependent photoluminance behavior as well as exhibited good transparency. Thus, the fabricated WPU nanocomposites show great potential as low VOC containing environment friendly transparent surface coating material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waterborne polymer
Hyperbranched polyurethane
Carbon dot nanocomposite
Performance
Surface coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CD and (b) FTIR spectrum of CD.
FTIR spectra of (a) WPU, (b) PNC0.25, (c) PNC0.5 and (d) PNC1.0.
UV–visible spectra of CD and nanocomposites.
XRD patterns of CD and nanocomposites.
Transparency of pristine polymer and nanocomposites.
Color of CD and nanocomposite films under different wavelengths of light.
Photoluminescence of (a) CD and (b) nanocomposites at different wavelengths.
(a) TGA thermograms and (b) DTA curves of WPU and nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Bio-based waterborne polyurethane/carbon dot nanocomposite as a surface coating material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Polymer and Nanomaterial Laboratory, Center for Polymer Science and Technology, Department of Chemical Sciences, Tezpur University, Napaam, Tezpur 784028, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Polymer and Nanomaterial Laboratory, Center for Polymer Science and Technology, Department of Chemical Sciences, Tezpur University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waterborne surface coating materials with low volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are environmentally friendly and hence the most desired materials for the modern society. The present study reports in situ fabricated thermosetting hyperbranched waterborne polyurethane (WPU)/carbon dot (CD) nanocomposites as surface coating materials. CD was used as a nanoreinforcing agent (at 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 weight percentages) in the nanocomposites. The structures of the waterborne polyurethane and its fabricated thermosetting nanocomposites were analyzed by using different spectroscopic and analytical tools like Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy and X-ray diffractometry. The mechanical properties of the pristine thermosetting WPU were significantly improved after incorporation of CD (tensile strength from 4.5 to 8.5MPa, elongation at break value from 96 to 136%, scratch hardness from 3 to 9kg, impact strength from 70 to 100cm). The thermal stability of WPU also enhanced from 250 to 280°C after incorporation of CD in the matrix. Interestingly, the fabricated nanocomposites demonstrated an excitation wavelength and concentration dependent photoluminance behavior as well as exhibited good transparency. Thus, the fabricated WPU nanocomposites show great potential as low VOC containing environment friendly transparent surface coating material.

ce:section.ce:para: Amongst all the polymers, polyurethane (PU) is one of the most important and versatile polymers. It is obtained by the rearrangement reaction between di/poly isocyanates and di/polyol compounds [1,2]. The promising feature of PU is that its properties can be tailor-made by varying its compositions, structures of raw materials and NCO/OH ratio [3,4]. In this context, hyperbranched polyurethane (HPU) is attracted much attention due to its several advantages such as low viscosity, high solubility etc. As a result, its processing is much easier than its linear counterpart [5]. However, such conventional solvent based PU possesses some serious problems such as high volatile organic compound content (VOC), flammability, toxicity etc. These problems can be effectively addressed by waterborne PUs (WPUs) which are binary colloidal system where stabilizing group containing PU particles dispersed in water medium. Generally, WPUs are prepared by reacting less reactive diisocyanate, polyol and ion generating dihydroxy acid such as 2, 2-bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid [6]. WPUs exhibit some advantages other than the above mentioned issues such as cost effectiveness and eco-friendly nature [7,8]. In spite of aforementioned advantages, WPUs also associate with some serious short comings like inferior mechanical property due to the presence of hydrophilic ionic groups or water soluble segments [9]. They are also moisture and other chemical environments sensitive. This causes inferior mechanical properties, thermal stability and poor chemical resistance as compared to solvent borne counterpart.

However, modifications like blending, creating a cross linking reaction or forming an interpenetrating network could be an option to improve the material properties of WPUs [10]. In addition to this, nanotechnology based strategy in the form of polymeric nanocomposite can effectively tackle the problem associate with WPUs. A numerous WPU nanocomposites are found in literature that exhibit dramatic enhancement in mechanical properties after incorporation of minute amount of nanofillers [11–16]. Different types of nano fillers like graphene oxides, zinc oxide, silver nanoparticles, nanoclay, functionalized carbon nanotubes, etc. are used for the fabrication of nanocomposites [17–20]. Various bionanofillers have also been utilized in this endeavor [13,21,22]. The improvement in properties of nanocomposites relies on the facts such as optimized dispersion, interfacial interactions and nanoscale morphology [23]. Among the genre of nanofillers, carbon based nanomaterial are engraved a distinct niche due to their high aspect ratio, high mechanical and thermal properties [14–16]. Even though this kind of carbon nanomaterials possesses such excellent properties, but they also suffer from some inadequacy such as agglomeration particularly at high dose level due to lack of proper functionality and inherent inertness. In this context, carbon dot (CD) is attracted the interest of material scientists in recent times due to its high aqueous solubility, facile modification, tunable fluorescence emissions, cheap production and excellent biological properties like good biocompatibility etc. [24–26]. Thus, fabrication of WPU/CD nanocomposite is a good choice for this purpose. Therefore, current study reports preparation of thermosetting WPU/CD nanocomposites by incorporating different weight% of CD following an in situ polymerization technique. A glycerol based polyester polyol has been utilized successfully for the development of hyperbranched polyurethane architecture. Thermosetting version of the polyurethane was obtained by curing with diglycidyl ether based epoxy and fatty acid based poly(amido amine). The mechanical, thermal and optical properties of the thermosetting nanocomposites were delved into for its utility as environmentally friendly low VOC surface coating material. Use of glycerol based polymer, fatty acid based poly(amido amine) and bio-based nanomaterial to fabricate a novel polymeric nanocomposite also justifies its bio-based attribute.

Isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, Aldrich, Germany), triethylamine (TEA, Merck, India) and quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) were used as received. Poly(ethylene glycol) with M
n
=600 and M
n
=200 (PEG-600, and PEG-200 Merck, India), butane diol (BD, Merck, India), 2,2-bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid (BMPA, Aldrich, Germany), glycerol (Merck, India) and maleic anhydride (MA, Merck, India) were kept in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 6h prior to use. Then it was kept in a vacuum desiccator for overnight. Banana (Musa acuminate) was purchased from local market of Tezpur, Assam, India. Epoxy resin like Araldite (Petro Araldite Pvt Ltd, India) and hardener like poly(amido amine) (PAA, Aradur of Petro Araldite Pvt Ltd, India) were also used as received. Epoxy resin and PAA were used for curing of WPU. Tetrahydrofuran (THF, Merck, India,) was used after distillation.

CD was prepared from banana juice by following the procedure described elsewhere [24]. Briefly, pulp free banana juice was heated at 150°C for 4h with ethanol. The dark brown product obtained was filtered and the filtrate was centrifuged with ethanol to separate the large particles. CD was obtained in a semi solid form after drying under reduced pressure.

WPU/CD nanocomposites were prepared by using an in situ pre-polymer process. In the first step required amount of IPDI (4.44g), PEG 600 (4.8g) and BMPA (0.7102g) (NCO to OH ratio is 1.5) were taken into a three neck 100mL round bottom flask equipped with a nitrogen inlet, a mechanical stirrer and a thermometer. The reaction was carried out at 80–85°C for 2h under constant mechanical agitation under nitrogen atmosphere. The reaction mass became highly viscous and white in color. It was cooled down to room temperature. Then 0.5g of the polyol and 0.2g of BD were dissolved in 15mL of THF and this solution was added into the reaction (NCO/OH ratio was equal to one) mixture. The reaction was carried out at 65–70°C for another 3.5h. In the last step, CD was introduced into the reaction and it was further heated at the same temperature for another 1.5h. TEA was then added drop wise to neutralize COOH groups at room temperature followed by addition of water slowly. THF was recovered under reduced pressure. WPU nanocomposite was mixed with epoxy (30wt%) and PAA (15wt%) by using mechanical stirring. Then the mixture was ultrasonicated to obtain a uniform homogeneous dispersion. This was cured at 100°C for 45min after degassing under vacuum to obtain the desired thermosetting nanocomposite. CD was incorporated into WPU matrix in 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0wt% separately by following the same procedure and coded as PNC0.25, PNC0.5, PNC1.0, respectively. A pristine thermosetting WPU was also prepared by the same method without using CD.

FTIR spectra were recorded in a Nicolet FTIR spectrophotometer (Impact-410 Madison, USA) using KBr pellet. The 1H NMR spectrum of WPU was recorded with 400MHz, Joel, Japan NMR spectrometer by using d 8-THF as the solvent and trimethyl silane as the internal standard. UV visible spectra were recorded at room temperature (25°C) using a Hitachi spectrophotometer (U2001, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) behavior was studied by using a PerkinElmer fluorescence spectrometer. The scratch hardness was carried out by scratch hardness tester (Sheen Instrument Ltd, UK) and impact strength by impact tester (S.C. Dey Co., Kolkata) of the cured film. Universal testing machine (UTM, Zwick Z010, Germany) equipped with a 500N load cell operated at a crosshead speed of 50mm/min was used to measure the tensile strength and elongation at break value. Gloss of the films was measured by mini gloss meter (Sheen Instruments Ltd, UK) at an incident angle 60°. The thermal degradation behavior of WPU and nanocomposite films was studied by thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) in a PerkinElmer, TGA-4000 thermal instrument using nitrogen flow rate of 30mL/min and at the heat rate of 10°C/min from room temperature to 700°C. The crystallinity pattern was studied by X-ray diffraction technique (XRD) at room temperature on a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (Miniflex, UK) in the range of 2–70°.

Polyester polyol was prepared by a two steps process. In the first step PEG 200 and MA (1:2 molar ratio) were reacted at 150°C for 2.5h in a three necked glass reactor equipped with a mechanical stirrer. In the second step, glycerol is added and reaction was carried out for 3h at 160°C. p-Toluene sulphonic acid was used as catalyst. The synthesized polyester polyol was characterized by FTIR and 1H NMR spectroscopy.

1
H NMR data (ppm): δ 6.6–6.8 (H CC H olefinic protons), δ 7.8 (O H hydroxyl proton), δ 4.3 (CH
2
OCO), δ 3.4 (CH
2
OH), 1.3–2.5 (CH
2
).

CD was prepared by heating pulp free banana juice at 150°C for 4h with ethanol. CD was obtained in a semisolid form. TEM image depicted in Fig. 1a reveals pseudo spherical morphology having dimension in between 4.15 and 6.60nm range. Thermosetting WPU/CD nanocomposites were fabricated by in situ polymerization technique by using CD as shown in Scheme 1 . CD contains various polar oxygenating functional groups viz. OH (3400cm−1), CO (1750cm−1), epoxy groups (1050cm−1 for COC) which are confirmed from the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b). These surface polar groups play a substantial role to interact with WPU matrix by covalently or non-covalently (H-bonding, polar–polar interactions etc.). These types of interactions govern the homogeneous distribution of CD in WPU matrix. In order to get thermosetting nanocomposite, it was mixed with epoxy and PAA and cured at elevated temperature. Different types of interactions present in the thermosetting nanocomposite are shown in Scheme 2 .

FTIR spectra of the thermosetting nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 2 . The presence of characteristic bands at 3422cm−1 (NH stretching vibration), 2950cm−1 (CH stretching), 1713cm−1 (CO stretching vibration), 1643cm−1 (CC stretching vibration), 1562cm−1 (NH bending vibration) and 1462cm−1(CO stretching vibration) indicate the various chemical linkages present in the nanocomposites [5,24]. A shifting of NH stretching vibration was observed to 3403cm−1 from 3422cm−1 after incorporation of CD into WPU matrix. CH stretching vibration as well as CO stretching vibration also shifted to lower values. This suggests presence of strong interactions and chemical crosslinking of CD with the polymer matrix [27].

IPDI possesses two isocyanates groups (NCO primary and NCO secondary) and is tranquil of two isomeric forms (Z and E) which cause interpretation of 1H NMR spectrum of the synthesized WPU difficult [8]. These two groups under different conditions show different selectivity toward urethane reaction. Nevertheless, 1H NMR spectrum of WPU as depicted in Fig. 3 provides a valuable information about the structure of prepared polymer. The mechanism of urethane reaction suggests that, secondary NCO group of IPDI has preferable tendency to participate in urethane reaction. Hence, NCO secondary is anticipated to react with OH functionality in the first step of the synthesis. The formation of cis and trans urethanes is confirmed by NH urethane peaks, which were observed at δ 7.2–7.4ppm. The important peaks observed for WPU are (a) δ 0.8–1.0 for [CH 2 ], (b) δ 1.58 for [CH
2
CH2 NH2, CH
2
CH2 CO], (c) δ 2.52 [N(CH
2 )2], (d) δ 2.7 [CH
2
NH], (e) δ 2.98 [CH
2
NH2], (f) δ 3.48 [CH
2
OH], (g) δ 3.6–3.8 for [CH 2 O], (h) δ 4.8–4.9 for [CH
2
OC] and (i) δ 5.10–5.12 [amine and hydroxyl proton] etc. Thus, it confirms the presence of urethane linkages in the structure of WPU [8,28].

UV-visible spectroscopic study was carried out to confirm the presence of CD in the fabricated nanocomposites. The UV-visible spectra of the nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 4 . In the spectrum of CD, an optical absorption peak was observed with maximum absorption at around 280nm [24]. In the spectra of the nanocomposites, the peak intensity of CD decreases after incorporation in the polymer matrix. This may be due to presence of less amount of CD in the nanocomposite as the intensity depends on concentration of CD.

XRD patterns of CD and thermosetting WPU/CD nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 5 . In the XRD patterns of the thermosetting nanocomposites, a diffraction peak was observed around 2θ =20° for CD [24]. It was found that the intensity of diffraction peak increased upon incorporation of CD into the polymer matrix. Strong interactions in between polar functional groups of CD and polymer matrix may be the reason for increment in the intensity [13].

All the thermosetting nanocomposites exhibit very interesting optical properties. It was observed that nanocomposites with most of the carbonaceous nanomaterials e.g. graphene oxide, carbon nanotube, etc. causes lose of transparency. However, the studied nanocomposite films even at the highest CD loading showed good transparency as shown in (Fig. 6 ). The reason behind this is the quantum size of CD and their uniform distribution over the polymer matrix. Proper solubility of both the nanomaterial and the polymer in the aqueous medium also contributed for good transparency.

All the WPU/CD nanocomposites exhibited excellent photoluminescence behavior when exposed under different wavelengths of light viz. visible, short UV (254nm) and long UV (365nm). The nanocomposite films exhibited different colors in different wavelength as shown in Fig. 6. The photoluminescence spectra are shown in Figs. 7 and 8
. The quantum yield of CD was found to be 7.21% as calculated at 360nm using quinine sulphate as the reference compound. With increase in excitation wavelength emission peak shifts toward higher wavelength. This may be due to the existence of different surface states and size dispersion of CD. Moreover, with increase in CD concentration intensity also gets enhanced. Compared to only CD, the nanocomposite films exhibited a much broader PL peak which can be attributed to inner filter effect. Hence, by incorporating CD attractive optical properties can be achieved which can be used in anticounterfiet and optoelectronics devices [29–32].

Mechanical properties of the thermosetting WPU/CD nanocomposites are tabulated in Table 1 . The mechanical properties of the nanocomposites are significantly enhanced even at low loading of CD compared to pristine WPU. The nanocomposites exhibited good tensile strength which increased with increasing concentration of CD. Scratch hardness and impact resistance of the nanocomposites were enhanced after incorporation of CD. Flexibility and elongation at break of the nanocomposites were also significantly improved by the incorporation of CD into the polymer matrix. It is one of the most exciting achievements as epoxy modified polyurethane thermoset generally suffer from low flexibility and elongation at break. The factors which can be ascribed for this improved performance of nanocomposites are excellent dispersion, strong interfacial interaction and nanoscale morphology of CD. Again, CD possesses carbonized core structure and polar functional groups which help to provide stiffness and strong interactions with the polymer matrix. As a result the nanocomposites exhibited improved mechanical properties [27].

Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to study the thermal behavior of the thermosetting WPU/CD nanocomposites (Fig. 9a). DTA curves shown in Fig. 9b reveals that degradation pattern follows almost single step process with maximum weight lost around 350–450°C. The initial degradation started in the temperature range of 250–280°C. This initial degradation temperature increases with the increase in loading of CD in the nanocomposites. This is due to the presence of CD with thermostable carbonized core structure which can also confer different types of physico-chemical interactions as well. The overall thermal degradation can be attributed to the degradation of thermo labile aliphatic moieties, urethane bonds and ester groups followed by degradation of cycloaliphatic ring of isocyanates. The good thermostability of the nanocomposites is due to the presence of various primary and secondary interactions imparted by CD with the polymer chain as well as high cross link density of the thermoset which make the system more rigid. These factors provide a restriction on the mobility of polymeric chains and hence, the thermal stability of the nanocomposite gets improved [32].

In conclusion, the present investigation showed that the presence of CD can significantly improve the properties of thermosetting WPU. After incorporation of small amount of CD, mechanical properties of WPU improved, most importantly elongation at break of the nanocomposite is increased. The fabricated nanocomposite also exhibited good thermal stability. The fabricated nanocomposite demonstrated good excitation wavelength as well as concentration dependent optical behavior and retains transparency even at high dose level of CD. Therefore, the fabricated thermoset WPU/CD nanocomposite shows potential as a surface coating material.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their acknowledgement toward DBT, India for the financial assistance through grant No. DBT-BT/235/NE/TBP/2011.
